items.content: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. What’s next for IVF IVF has brought millions of babies into the world over the last four decades. But the process can still be slow, painful, and expensive—and far from guaranteed…

items.title: The balcony solar boom is coming to the US
items.content: Dozens of US states are considering legislation to allow people to install plug-in solar systems, often called balcony solar. These small arrays require little to no setup and could help cut emissions and power bills. Balcony solar is already popular in Europe, and proponents say that the systems could make solar power more accessible for…

LIC’s $2 billion contrarian bet: 10 stocks the DII giant bought while the market bled
items.content: Life Insurance Corporation of India invested nearly $2 billion in 10 stocks during the March quarter's market fall. LIC bought shares in companies like Bajaj Finance, Bharti Airtel, TCS, and Infosys even as their prices dropped. This strategic move saw LIC increase its stake in several companies. The insurer also reduced holdings in other stocks, showing a selective approach.

items.title: US Stock Market: Inflation expectations remain stable despite energy price surge: Fed’s Mary Daly
items.content: Federal Reserve's Mary Daly affirmed the central bank's commitment to its 2% inflation target, stating that rising energy prices have not yet impacted medium- or long-term inflation expectations. She emphasized that the Fed's current monetary policy is 'slightly restrictive' and that a resolution to the U.S.-Iran conflict could further ease inflationary pressures.

There is no vaccine for deadly hantavirus: what that means for future outbreaks
Scientists accidentally discover DNA that breaks the rules of life
items.content: A routine experiment with a new single-cell DNA sequencing method turned into a surprising scientific twist when researchers stumbled upon a bizarre genetic code in a microscopic pond organism. Instead of following the near-universal “rules” of life, this newly identified protist rewrites how genes signal their end. This unexpected discovery challenges long-held assumptions about how genetic translation works and hints that nature may be far more flexible—and mysterious—than scientists realized.
items.summary: A routine experiment with a new single-cell DNA sequencing method turned into a surprising scientific twist when researchers stumbled upon a bizarre genetic code in a microscopic pond organism. Instea...

items.title: New “Trojan horse” obesity drug supercharges weight loss in early tests
items.content: Researchers have created a next-generation obesity drug that works like a “Trojan horse,” using GLP-1/GIP signals to slip a powerful metabolic enhancer directly into target cells. In mice, it outperformed existing treatments—curbing appetite, increasing weight loss, and improving blood sugar levels. Because the extra drug acts only where it’s needed, it can be used at much lower doses, potentially reducing side effects.
